([/kəˈluːjə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born 24 February 1989) is a British actor.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) His work [encompasses both screen and stage](/wiki/Daniel_Kaluuya_on_screen_and_stage "Daniel Kaluuya on screen and stage"), and [his accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Daniel_Kaluuya "List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Kaluuya") include an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), two [BAFTAs](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), an [Actor Award](/wiki/Actor_Awards "Actor Awards"), and a [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), in addition to nominations for a [Laurence Olivier Award](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier_Awards "Laurence Olivier Awards") and an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards"). In 2021, he was named among the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British actor (born 1989)

| Daniel Kaluuya | |
| --- | --- |
| Kaluuya at the 2017 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") | |
| Born | (1989-02-24) 24 February 1989 (age 37)  [London](/wiki/London "London"), England |
| Occupations | * Actor * writer * director * producer |
| Years active | 2006–present |
| Works | [Full list](/wiki/Daniel_Kaluuya_on_screen_and_stage "Daniel Kaluuya on screen and stage") |
| Awards | [Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Daniel_Kaluuya "List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Kaluuya") |

**Daniel Kaluuya** ([/kəˈluːjə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born 24 February 1989) is a British actor.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) His work [encompasses both screen and stage](/wiki/Daniel_Kaluuya_on_screen_and_stage "Daniel Kaluuya on screen and stage"), and [his accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Daniel_Kaluuya "List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Kaluuya") include an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), two [BAFTAs](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), an [Actor Award](/wiki/Actor_Awards "Actor Awards"), and a [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), in addition to nominations for a [Laurence Olivier Award](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier_Awards "Laurence Olivier Awards") and an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards"). In 2021, he was named among the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Kaluuya began his acting career as a teenager in [improvisational theatre](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre").[[4]](#cite_note-Bustle-4) He played [Posh Kenneth](/wiki/Posh_Kenneth "Posh Kenneth") in the first two seasons of the television series *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")* (2007–2009); he also co-wrote some of the episodes.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Kaluuya drew praise for his leading performance in *[Sucker Punch](/wiki/Sucker_Punch_(play) "Sucker Punch (play)")* at the [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre") in 2010.[[6]](#cite_note-6) He went on to gain attention for his television roles in *[Psychoville](/wiki/Psychoville "Psychoville")* (2009–2011), *[The Fades](/wiki/The_Fades_(TV_series) "The Fades (TV series)")* (2011), and the *[Black Mirror](/wiki/Black_Mirror "Black Mirror")* episode "[Fifteen Million Merits](/wiki/Fifteen_Million_Merits "Fifteen Million Merits")" (2011).[[7]](#cite_note-7) He also had supporting roles in the films *[Johnny English Reborn](/wiki/Johnny_English_Reborn "Johnny English Reborn")* (2011), *[Kick-Ass 2](/wiki/Kick-Ass_2_(film) "Kick-Ass 2 (film)")* (2013), and *[Sicario](/wiki/Sicario_(2015_film) "Sicario (2015 film)")* (2015).

In 2017, Kaluuya had his [breakthrough](/wiki/Breakthrough_role "Breakthrough role") starring in [Jordan Peele](/wiki/Jordan_Peele "Jordan Peele")'s horror film *[Get Out](/wiki/Get_Out "Get Out")*, which garnered him a nomination for the [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor").[[8]](#cite_note-8) This was followed by roles in [Ryan Coogler](/wiki/Ryan_Coogler "Ryan Coogler")'s superhero film *[Black Panther](/wiki/Black_Panther_(film) "Black Panther (film)")* (2018), [Steve McQueen](/wiki/Steve_McQueen_(director) "Steve McQueen (director)")'s crime drama *[Widows](/wiki/Widows_(2018_film) "Widows (2018 film)")* (2018), Peele's horror film *[Nope](/wiki/Nope_(film) "Nope (film)")* (2022), and [Sony Pictures Animation](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Animation "Sony Pictures Animation")'s animated superhero film *[Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Across_the_Spider-Verse "Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse")* (2023). For his portrayal of [Black Panther Party](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party") leader [Fred Hampton](/wiki/Fred_Hampton "Fred Hampton") in the biopic *[Judas and the Black Messiah](/wiki/Judas_and_the_Black_Messiah "Judas and the Black Messiah")* (2021), he won the [BAFTA](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Actor_in_a_Supporting_Role "BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Supporting Role") and [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) He has since co-directed the drama *[The Kitchen](/wiki/The_Kitchen_(2023_film) "The Kitchen (2023 film)")* (2023).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Kaluuya&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Kaluuya was born on 24 February 1989[[11]](#cite_note-11) in London to [Ugandan](/wiki/Ugandans_in_Britain "Ugandans in Britain") parents, who are [Basoga](/wiki/Basoga "Basoga") and [Baganda](/wiki/Baganda "Baganda") from [Makindye Division](/wiki/Makindye_Division "Makindye Division").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) His mother[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Vice-15) raised him on a [council estate](/wiki/Public_housing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Public housing in the United Kingdom") in [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town"), along with an older sister. His father lived in [Balaka](/wiki/Balaka_Township,_Malawi "Balaka Township, Malawi"), Malawi, and they had no contact until he was 14.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Kaluuya attended [Torriano Primary School](/wiki/Torriano_Primary_School "Torriano Primary School"), and [St Aloysius' College, Highgate](/wiki/St_Aloysius%27_College,_Highgate "St Aloysius' College, Highgate").[[15]](#cite_note-Vice-15) He subsequently took [A-level](/wiki/A-level "A-level") History, Drama and Biology at [Camden School for Girls](/wiki/Camden_School_for_Girls "Camden School for Girls") in its co-educational sixth-form.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Kaluuya wrote his first play at the age of nine, after which he began performing [improvisational theatre](/wiki/Improvisational_theatre "Improvisational theatre").[[4]](#cite_note-Bustle-4) He began acting as a child at his local [Anna Scher Theatre](/wiki/Anna_Scher_Theatre "Anna Scher Theatre") School and WAC Arts.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Kaluuya&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 2006–2009: Career beginnings and *Skins*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Kaluuya&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2006–2009: Career beginnings and Skins")]

Kaluuya appeared in his first credited acting role in 2006 as Reece in the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s controversial drama *[Shoot the Messenger](/wiki/Shoot_the_Messenger_(film) "Shoot the Messenger (film)")*.[[4]](#cite_note-Bustle-4)[[15]](#cite_note-Vice-15) Kaluuya then joined the original cast of *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")* as Posh Kenneth; he was also a contributing writer on the first two seasons of the series, as well as the head writer of the episodes titled "Jal" and "Thomas".[[19]](#cite_note-19)

After *Skins*, Kaluuya appeared as a guest star in many popular television series such as *[Silent Witness](/wiki/Silent_Witness "Silent Witness")*, the *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* special "[Planet of the Dead](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Dead "Planet of the Dead")", and *[Lewis](/wiki/Lewis_(TV_series) "Lewis (TV series)")*. He has also appeared in the sketch show *[That Mitchell and Webb Look](/wiki/That_Mitchell_and_Webb_Look "That Mitchell and Webb Look")* twice and as fan favourite character "Parking Pataweyo" in the sketch show *[Harry & Paul](/wiki/Harry_%26_Paul "Harry & Paul")*. Kaluuya also voiced a character in the [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4") sitcom *[Sneakiepeeks](/wiki/Sneakiepeeks "Sneakiepeeks")*.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 2009, he became a regular cast member in the ITV comedy *[FM](/wiki/FM_(British_TV_series) "FM (British TV series)")*. At the end of 2009, the *Screen International Magazine* picked Kaluuya out in their annual report as a *UK Star of Tomorrow*.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### 2010–2018: Stage, *Get Out*, and *Black Panther*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Kaluuya&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2010–2018: Stage, Get Out, and Black Panther")]

In 2010, Kaluuya played the lead role in [Roy Williams](/wiki/Roy_Williams_(playwright) "Roy Williams (playwright)")' *[Sucker Punch](/wiki/Sucker_Punch_(play) "Sucker Punch (play)")* at the [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre") in London; Kaluuya won rave reviews for his performance and he won both the [Evening Standard Award](/wiki/Evening_Standard_Theatre_Awards "Evening Standard Theatre Awards") and [Critics' Circle Theatre Award](/wiki/Critics%27_Circle_Theatre_Award "Critics' Circle Theatre Award") for Outstanding Newcomer.[[22]](#cite_note-Film_Independent-22) From 2009 to 2011, he portrayed Michael "Tea Leaf" Fry in the dark BBC comedy *[Psychoville](/wiki/Psychoville "Psychoville")*.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/TIFF_2018_%2844582000541%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-TIFF_2018_%2844582000541%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TIFF_2018_(44582000541)_(cropped).jpg)

Kaluuya in 2018

Between 2011 and 2013, Kaluuya appeared in several short films, most notably in [Daniel Mulloy](/wiki/Daniel_Mulloy "Daniel Mulloy")'s *[Baby](/wiki/Baby_(2010_film) "Baby (2010 film)")*, which premiered at the [Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/Sundance_Film_Festival "Sundance Film Festival"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) and went on to win the Best Short Film Award at the [Edinburgh International Film Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Film_Festival "Edinburgh International Film Festival"), as well as the Best Short Film Award at the [British Independent Film Awards](/wiki/British_Independent_Film_Awards "British Independent Film Awards").[[22]](#cite_note-Film_Independent-22) In 2011, he appeared in the sequel to the 2003 film *[Johnny English](/wiki/Johnny_English "Johnny English")*, titled *[Johnny English Reborn](/wiki/Johnny_English_Reborn "Johnny English Reborn")*, as Agent Tucker.[[22]](#cite_note-Film_Independent-22) Also in 2011, he played the role of Mac Armstrong in [BBC3](/wiki/BBC_Three "BBC Three")'s [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") drama series *[The Fades](/wiki/The_Fades_(TV_series) "The Fades (TV series)")*.[[22]](#cite_note-Film_Independent-22) In 2013, he appeared in the [superhero](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") [comedy film](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film") *[Kick Ass 2](/wiki/Kick-Ass_2_(film) "Kick-Ass 2 (film)")*.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 2015, he portrayed an [FBI Agent](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") in [Denis Villeneuve](/wiki/Denis_Villeneuve "Denis Villeneuve")'s thriller film *[Sicario](/wiki/Sicario_(2015_film) "Sicario (2015 film)")*.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Kaluuya played one of the lead characters opposite [Jessica Brown Findlay](/wiki/Jessica_Brown_Findlay "Jessica Brown Findlay") in "[Fifteen Million Merits](/wiki/Fifteen_Million_Merits "Fifteen Million Merits")", an episode of the [anthology series](/wiki/Anthology_series "Anthology series") *[Black Mirror](/wiki/Black_Mirror "Black Mirror")*, for which he received positive reviews from critics.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The episode originally premiered on [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") in 2011, but gained popularity after it was subsequently released on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") in the United States.[[29]](#cite_note-29) It was his performance in *Black Mirror* that attracted the attention of [Jordan Peele](/wiki/Jordan_Peele "Jordan Peele"), who later cast him in *[Get Out](/wiki/Get_Out "Get Out")*, which proved to be his breakthrough role.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Kaluuya's performance in *Get Out*, which was released in cinemas on 24 February 2017,[[32]](#cite_note-32) attracted significant critical acclaim.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-variety_1202647420-34) Steven Gaydos of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* wrote that "the terror, tension, humor, and fury of this powerfully effective cinematic balancing act all rests on the shoulders of this brilliant young British actor who communicates universal anxieties without ever losing the essential home address of his beleaguered African-American hero."[[34]](#cite_note-variety_1202647420-34) [Richard Lawson](/wiki/Richard_Lawson_(writer) "Richard Lawson (writer)") of *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")* called Kaluuya's performance a "masterful, telling piece of acting."[[35]](#cite_note-35) For his performance, he received [numerous accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Daniel_Kaluuya "List of awards and nominations received by Daniel Kaluuya"), including an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") nomination for [Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor"). He also received nominations for a [BAFTA Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"),[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Critics' Choice Award](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Movie_Awards "Critics' Choice Movie Awards"),[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) and [SAG Award](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Awards "Screen Actors Guild Awards")[[39]](#cite_note-39) for his role. In 2018, he received the [BAFTA Rising Star Award](/wiki/BAFTA_Rising_Star_Award "BAFTA Rising Star Award").[[40]](#cite_note-BAFTA_noms-40)

In December 2016, Kaluuya was cast in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") film *[Black Panther](/wiki/Black_Panther_(film) "Black Panther (film)")*.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The film was released on 16 February 2018, in the United States.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Kaluuya also had a large role in the heist film *[Widows](/wiki/Widows_(2018_film) "Widows (2018 film)")* (2018), and starred in the road trip/crime movie *[Queen & Slim](/wiki/Queen_%26_Slim "Queen & Slim")* (2019), opposite [Jodie Turner-Smith](/wiki/Jodie_Turner-Smith "Jodie Turner-Smith").

In 2018, he joined the voice cast of the BBC and Netflix's miniseries *[Watership Down](/wiki/Watership_Down_(2018_TV_series) "Watership Down (2018 TV series)")*.[[43]](#cite_note-THR-43)

### 2019–present: *Nope*, *Judas*, *Barney*, and *The Kitchen*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Kaluuya&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2019–present: Nope, Judas, Barney, and The Kitchen")]

In October 2019, it was announced that Kaluuya was to produce a feature film based on the children's television show *[Barney & Friends](/wiki/Barney_%26_Friends "Barney & Friends")* through his newly formed [production company](/wiki/Production_company "Production company") 59%, alongside [Mattel Films](/wiki/Mattel_Films "Mattel Films"). According to Kaluuya, the *Barney* film would "surprise audiences and subvert expectations."[[44]](#cite_note-44) In February 2025, it was announced that [Ayo Edebiri](/wiki/Ayo_Edebiri "Ayo Edebiri") was in talks to star in and write the film for [A24](/wiki/A24 "A24").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In 2020, Kaluuya was announced to produce and star in an adaptation of [Femi Fadugba](/wiki/Femi_Fadugba "Femi Fadugba")'s debut novel *The Upper World* at [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"). In 2021, he starred as [revolutionary socialist](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism") [Fred Hampton](/wiki/Fred_Hampton "Fred Hampton") in the biographical drama *[Judas and the Black Messiah](/wiki/Judas_and_the_Black_Messiah "Judas and the Black Messiah")*. His performance in the film was lauded by critics,[[46]](#cite_note-46) winning the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor"), [BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Supporting Role](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Actor_in_a_Supporting_Role "BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Supporting Role"), [Critics' Choice Movie Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Movie_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Critics' Choice Movie Award for Best Supporting Actor"), [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") and [Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by a Male Actor in a Supporting Role](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Award_for_Outstanding_Performance_by_a_Male_Actor_in_a_Supporting_Role "Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by a Male Actor in a Supporting Role");[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) becoming the only performer that year to win [all five major film awards](/wiki/2020_in_film#Awards "2020 in film"). At age 32, Daniel Kaluuya became the [seventh-youngest](/wiki/List_of_oldest_and_youngest_Academy_Award_winners_and_nominees#Best_Actor_in_a_Supporting_Role "List of oldest and youngest Academy Award winners and nominees") Academy Award Best Supporting Actor winner.

In early 2021, Kaluuya was cast in Peele's sci-fi horror [*Nope*](/wiki/Nope_(film) "Nope (film)"), alongside [Keke Palmer](/wiki/Keke_Palmer "Keke Palmer") and [Steven Yeun](/wiki/Steven_Yeun "Steven Yeun"). The film released on 22 July 2022 to positive reviews.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) Due to scheduling conflicts with *Nope*, it was announced that Kaluuya will not be reprising his role as [W'Kabi](/wiki/W%27Kabi "W'Kabi") in *Black Panther* sequel, *[Black Panther: Wakanda Forever](/wiki/Black_Panther:_Wakanda_Forever "Black Panther: Wakanda Forever")*.[[52]](#cite_note-52) In April, Kaluuya hosted an episode of *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*, with musical guest [St. Vincent](/wiki/St._Vincent_(musician) "St. Vincent (musician)").[[53]](#cite_note-53) Kaluuya was also included in *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")*'s 2021 list of the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Daniel_Kaluuya_%26_Keke_Palmer_in_2022_01.jpg/250px-Daniel_Kaluuya_%26_Keke_Palmer_in_2022_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daniel_Kaluuya_%26_Keke_Palmer_in_2022_01.jpg)

Kaluuya and *Nope* co-star [Keke Palmer](/wiki/Keke_Palmer "Keke Palmer") in 2022.

In March 2022, it was revealed that Kaluuya was writing a [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian "Dystopian") drama for [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"), titled *[The Kitchen](/wiki/The_Kitchen_(2023_film) "The Kitchen (2023 film)")*, with [Kibwe Tavares](/wiki/Kibwe_Tavares "Kibwe Tavares") set to direct.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The drama will star [Kane Robinson](/wiki/Kane_Robinson "Kane Robinson") and Jedaiah Bannerman, and is being co-written by Joe Murtagh, and executive-produced by [Michael Fassbender](/wiki/Michael_Fassbender "Michael Fassbender").[[56]](#cite_note-56) In August 2023, the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") announced that the film would have its premiere as the closing gala of the 67th [London Film Festival](/wiki/BFI_London_Film_Festival "BFI London Film Festival") on 15 October 2023.[[57]](#cite_note-57) *The Kitchen* released on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") on 19 January 2024.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In May 2022, it was announced that Kaluuya was the narrator for [Amazon Prime](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video")'s sports [docuseries](/wiki/Docuseries "Docuseries") *[All or Nothing: Arsenal](/wiki/All_or_Nothing:_Arsenal "All or Nothing: Arsenal")*, which followed English [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") side [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") behind the scenes throughout their [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Arsenal_F.C._season "2021–22 Arsenal F.C. season").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) In November 2022, Kaluuya was confirmed to portray [Hobart "Hobie" Brown / Spider-Punk](/wiki/Spider-Punk "Spider-Punk") in *[Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Across_the_Spider-Verse "Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse")*.[[61]](#cite_note-SMATSV-61)

In October 2024, a statue of Kaluuya was erected in London to honour his work in *Get Out*.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In July 2025, it was reported that Kaluuya would co-write the screenplay for an animated Spider-Punk spin-off film.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Kaluuya&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Kaluuya lives in [West London](/wiki/West_London "West London").[[64]](#cite_note-64) He is a supporter of [Arsenal F.C.](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), to the extent of referring to Arsenal's North London rivals, [Tottenham Hotspur F.C.](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C."), as the "team who must not be named" and the "[Voldemort](/wiki/Lord_Voldemort "Lord Voldemort") of the League".[[65]](#cite_note-65) In August 2022, Kaluuya featured as narrator in *[All or Nothing: Arsenal](/wiki/All_or_Nothing:_Arsenal "All or Nothing: Arsenal")*, the [docuseries](/wiki/Docuseries "Docuseries") about [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")'s [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Arsenal_F.C._season "2021–22 Arsenal F.C. season").[[66]](#cite_note-66) He is a [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian") and acknowledged his faith during his [Oscar speech](/wiki/Oscar_speech "Oscar speech").[[67]](#cite_note-67) He is the [godfather](/wiki/Godparent "Godparent") of the son of his former *Skins* castmate [Kaya Scodelario](/wiki/Kaya_Scodelario "Kaya Scodelario").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In February 2022, it was reported that Kaluuya had "abruptly" fired his [agents](/wiki/Talent_agent "Talent agent") at the [Creative Artists Agency](/wiki/Creative_Artists_Agency "Creative Artists Agency") as well as his [personal stylist](/wiki/Personal_stylist "Personal stylist"), [publicist](/wiki/Publicist "Publicist") and multiple assistants. It was reported that he was influenced in these decisions by a person known as "Heir Holiness" who described herself as Kaluuya's "personal manager" and "life strategist" as well as the "Head Mistress" of "The International Alma Mater, Blessed University."[[69]](#cite_note-talismans-69)[[70]](#cite_note-HHP6-70) She has further claimed to have degrees in [holistic health](/wiki/Holistic_health "Holistic health") and "Parapsychic Science" from the American Institute of Holistic Theology. Sources told [Page Six](/wiki/Page_Six "Page Six") and [Matthew Belloni](/wiki/Matthew_Belloni "Matthew Belloni") that she was on set during the filming of *Nope* and her behavior "caused people on set to be concerned about her influence over" Kaluuya. However, in a March 2022 article, she denied to Page Six that she knew Kaluuya.[[70]](#cite_note-HHP6-70)
